old_claim.note: Precision Map Engraving provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Patent drawings translate an invention into referenced figures inside the specification, so technical drawing conventions enable the disclosure without making map engraving a prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Patent specification and drawings sections: the factsheet explains that drawings are referred to by the specification and use figures plus reference numbers or letters tied to the description.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source documents patent drawings as a specification-linked technical disclosure format rather than a cartographic engraving practice.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the corrected edge only needs a general technical-figure dependency, referenced figures in patent specifications support technical drawing standards and do not support map engraving as a prerequisite.

ontology_before: Patent Specification Drawings depended directly on Precision Map Engraving, implying a cartographic print-production pathway for patent disclosure drawings.
ontology_after: Patent Specification Drawings depend on Patent Systems and Technical Drawing Standards, with map engraving left as a separate cartographic reproduction technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: patent_specification_drawings now depends on technical_drawing_standards and no longer directly depends on precision_map_engraving.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to engraved map plates used as patent exhibits rather than formal patent specification drawings.
A source showed early patent drawings specifically required map engraving techniques as a direct prerequisite.
The node were broadened to any illustration attached to an invention privilege regardless of whether a patent specification was filed.
short_reason: Patent drawings are specification-linked technical figures, not map engraving.
